role_summary: First field secretary of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC); pastor of Mount Canaan Baptist Church in Shreveport, Louisiana (1966-2020); president of the Louisiana Baptist State Convention; general secretary of the National Baptist Convention USA, Inc; SCLC organizer from 1960.
ai_reading: Blake's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 77), with Charles McLaurin (entry 17) as the top match and the broader cluster including James Oscar Jones (entry 57), Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones (entry 92), and Wyatt Tee Walker (entry 132, the SCLC chief of staff). The Walker pairing is the catalog's clearest SCLC-field-secretary-and-headquarters cosine, fitting the cultural placement of Blake as SCLC's first field secretary (1960). The catalog flags a +0.78 reading on the Individual Conscience vs Collective Discipline axis (far toward Collective Discipline) as the most-distinctive structural reading; for a pastor and SCLC field secretary whose entire career was institutional church leadership, the embedding's pull toward Collective Discipline is consistent with the cultural placement, the catalog's most-confirming-alignment reading on a placeholder bio so far.
biographical_paragraph: While Blake's LoC oral history carries a not-auditable audit tier (the catalog cannot independently certify the transcript against primary sources for this entry, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), his external biographical record is documented in the Louisiana local press at the time of his April 2020 death [src: 1, src: 2]. Blake was born May 20, 1934 in Bossier Parish, Louisiana. He attended Bishop College in Marshall, Texas, where he heard Martin Luther King Jr. speak and joined the Southern Christian Leadership Conference in 1960 as the organization's first field secretary [src: 2]. Following the SCLC's October 1960 annual convention in Shreveport, a gunman attempted to kill him as he drove home, firing bullets through the windshield of his car [src: 2]. In early autumn 1963, after the September 15 Birmingham 16th Street Baptist Church bombing that killed four girls, Blake helped organize a memorial service at Little Union Baptist Church in Shreveport; he was beaten by several Shreveport police officers during the gathering and required stitches for cuts to his scalp [src: 2]. In 1966 he became pastor of Mount Canaan Baptist Church in Shreveport, the post he would hold for the remainder of his ministry. He served as president of the Louisiana Baptist State Convention and as general secretary of the National Baptist Convention USA, Inc. He died April 8, 2020 in Shreveport at eighty-five [src: 2].

sources.title: Reverend Harry Blake oral history
